preamble: The shift from a lateral approximant [l] to a nasal [n] often occurs through dissimilation (avoiding two 'l' sounds in one word) or via dialectal shifts and historical sound laws. While less common than the reverse (n -> l), it is well documented across multiple language families.

examples.note: Dissimilation to avoid two 'l's (via Old French 'livel').
Historical law shifting Proto-Algonquian liquid to nasal.
